title: Students Build a Giant Old Computer!
summary:
Imagine building a computer that fills an entire room! That's exactly what a group of students in Arizona did. They built a full-size replica of ENIAC, one of the very first electronic computers ever made.

ENIAC was built in the 1940s. It was about a thousand times faster than other machines at the time. But it was huge—it took up a whole room and used thousands of vacuum tubes (like old-fashioned light bulbs).

The teacher who led the project is Tom Burick. He teaches technology at PS Academy in Gilbert, Arizona, a school for students with autism and other special learning needs. Burick knows what it's like to learn differently—he has dyscalculia, which makes traditional math hard. But he says it's actually a superpower because it helps him think in 3D and solve problems in creative ways.

Burick's students worked together to build the replica for the 80th anniversary of ENIAC. They used their strengths, like being able to focus intensely and repeat tasks precisely. "I didn't want my technology program to feel like craft hour," Burick says. Instead, they built something real and historic.

Burick used to own a robotics company, but after it closed, he decided to become a teacher. He wanted to "pay forward" the help he got from mentors when he was young. Now he's inspiring a new generation of builders.
why_it_matters: This story shows that everyone has unique strengths—even a challenge like dyscalculia can become a superpower—and that working together can create something historic.

keywords.explanation: Old-fashioned light-bulb-like parts that early computers used to work.
A learning challenge that makes it hard for someone to do math or understand numbers.
An exact copy of something, built again.
One of the very first electronic computers, so big it filled a whole room.
A way of thinking and learning that can make someone great at focusing and repeating tasks.
Experienced people who give advice and help to someone younger or newer.

options: One of the first electronic computers

questions.question: How big was the original ENIAC?

background_read: ENIAC was built in 1945 at the University of Pennsylvania; it was used to calculate artillery tables for the U.S. Army and was one of the first electronic general-purpose computers.
PS Academy (the school in Gilbert, Arizona) is a place that helps students with autism and other special learning needs feel supported and learn in their own way.
Vacuum tubes were the technology before computer chips; ENIAC used about 18,000 of them and they often burned out.
Article_Structure: WHO: Teacher Tom Burick and his students at PS Academy in Gilbert, Arizona.
WHAT: They built a full-size working replica of ENIAC, an early computer from the 1940s.
WHERE: At PS Academy, a school for students with autism and other special needs in Arizona.
WHY: To celebrate ENIAC's 80th anniversary and show that students with learning differences can do amazing things.

perspectives.description: A student in the project feels proud that their ability to focus and repeat tasks helped build a real piece of history.
Tom Burick believes that turning his own math challenge into a 3D-thinking strength lets him inspire kids who learn differently.
Historians appreciate that students are learning computer history by actually building a machine, not just reading about it.
